root_cause_candidates.cause: Tail-latency spike and degraded performance in ts-order-service, with downstream impact propagating to ts-preserve-service. Downstream service exhibits elevated latency (p99 Δms ~283ms) and related anomalies, suggesting ts-order-service as the root fault causing observed delay in the symptom service.
root_cause_candidates.reasoning: All sources converge on ts-order-service as the likely upstream fault causing the observed delay in ts-preserve-service. The Log Agent points to a tail-latency spike in ts-order-service driving downstream delays. The Topology Agent confirms a direct dependency path ts-preserve-service <- ts-order-service and a plausible propagation. The TCB-RCA output does not align with this conclusion (it identifies ts-preserve-service as the root with no anomalies), so we override it in favor of the stronger, corroborated signals from log and topology and treat ts-order-service as the more credible root cause. The unobserved root-cause rule is not triggered here because ts-order-service itself shows observable error metrics (5 errors in ts-consign-mongo) and latency anomalies, indicating a measurable fault rather than an unmonitored deeper service.

final_verdict.cause: Tail-latency spike and degraded performance in ts-order-service, with downstream impact propagating to ts-preserve-service. Downstream service exhibits elevated latency (p99 Δms ~283ms) and related anomalies, suggesting ts-order-service as the root fault causing observed delay in the symptom service.

notes: Candidate 'ts-order-service' supported by 1 modality (has_log_evidence); confidence capped at 0.6.

LLM hypothesis: A tail-latency spike in ts-order-service (p99 Δms ~283ms) is driving downstream delays and cascading into ts-preserve-service; other services show correlated metric anomalies suggesting cascading impact from the order/service path.

metadata.hypothesis: A tail-latency spike in ts-order-service (p99 Δms ~283ms) is driving downstream delays and cascading into ts-preserve-service; other services show correlated metric anomalies suggesting cascading impact from the order/service path.
metadata.llm_reasoning: Metric Summary shows strongest anomalies in ts-order-service (high p99 latency and notable p95 as well as elevated CPU). Other services (ts-inside-payment-service, ts-food-map-service, ts-auth-service) also exhibit elevated metrics but to lesser extremes. No explicit upstream error logs are provided, so the deepest root is inferred from metrics with ts-order-service as the primary anomalous node and a downstream impact expectation on ts-preserve-service. Earliest/primary anomaly is thus centered on ts-order-service.

metadata.llm_reasoning: The symptom service ts-preserve-service directly depends on ts-order-service, forming the explicit path ts-preserve-service -> ts-order-service. This aligns with a plausible propagation where a fault in a downstream service (ts-order-service) could influence the symptom service through the dependency chain. No alternative indirect paths are evident from the given topology since we only have the explicit dependency from ts-preserve-service to ts-order-service. The blast radius is limited to the symptom service, given the current computed propagation path.
